questions.question: What is the one job of a generative model, and why is it fundamentally hard?
options: Given samples from an unknown distribution p_data(x), output new samples from the same distribution — hard because the space has millions of dimensions (a 512x512 RGB image is ~786k), real data sits on a thin manifold, and you have maybe 10M examples
Predicting the next label in a stream
questions.explanation: Brute-forcing the density is hopeless, so every generative family is a compromise that trades one hard problem for a slightly less hard one.

questions.question: What are the five surviving families of generative models?
options: Explicit-tractable density (autoregressive, flows), explicit-approximate (VAE, diffusion via ELBO), implicit (GANs), score-based/continuous-time (score matching, flow matching), and token-based autoregressive over discrete codes (VQ tokenizer + Transformer)
questions.explanation: Each bucket's compromise predicts its strengths: exact likelihood but slow sampling (bucket 1), unstable training but one-pass inference (GANs), dominant quality but iterative sampling (diffusion).

questions.question: Why did flow matching become the 2024-2026 default for new image models?
options: It was the first method invented
Simulate-free training with straighter probability paths gives 4-10x faster sampling than DDPM — Stable Diffusion 3, Flux, and AudioCraft 2 all use it
questions.explanation: Flow matching (Lipman 2023) sits in the score-based/continuous-time bucket — the SDE generalization of diffusion with better training dynamics and fewer sampling steps.

questions.question: Where do GANs still win in 2026, despite diffusion's dominance?
options: They don't — GANs are fully obsolete
Fixed-domain photorealism: StyleGAN 1/2/3 remain state of the art for faces and similar narrow domains, and one-forward-pass inference stays unbeatable for speed
questions.explanation: The implicit-density compromise — no likelihood, adversarial training instability — bought sharpness and speed. On a narrow manifold like faces, that trade still pays.

questions.question: What is the five-question triage for reading a new generative-model paper?
options: What is being modeled (pixels/latents/tokens/Gaussians)? Explicit or implicit density? One-shot or iterative sampling? What conditioning (none/class/text/image)? Which evaluation (FID/CLIP/human) — each with known failure modes?
Loss, optimizer, scheduler, batch size, seed
questions.explanation: Answering these before the method section places the paper in the taxonomy — and the bucket tells you what will be easy, slow, unstable, or impossible about it.
